⚠️ The chart is missing something for question 2: No structured symptom or nail/joint review is recorded at the February or March visits, so the nail and scalp findings can only be timed from the May visit onward.

⚠️ The chart is missing something for question 4: The chart never states which medication the patient is on, when it started, or whether doses were actually taken — the adherence review is filed separately and not available here.

Our evidence packet is ready — handing it to Agent B for review.
Round 1: adversarial review
We're checking Agent A's evidence against the rubric. We never see the chart ourselves — only what A quoted, plus outside records like the pharmacy's if we need them.
Ver
ification notes: The severity trend holds up — four d
ated visits with PASI 6.1→6.3→14.2→16.8, BSA and IGA stepping up, plus corroborating photos. DL
QI (7→8→15→17) and the new nail/scalp findings from May onward are documented with
quotes. But criteria 4 and 5 are both marked insufficient, and the whole worsening att
ribution rests on an assumption nobody has verified: that the patient was actually taking the biologic during the inter
val when he got worse. The chart defers adherence to a separate review not in the packet. The s
harp deterioration lands between the March and May visits —
I'm pulling the pharmacy fills to see whether coverage was continuous across that gap, because if it wasn't, non-adherence is an unexcluded confounder for the most recent worsening.

Verification notes: The severity trend holds up — four dated visits with PASI 6.1→6.3→14.2→16.8, BSA and IGA stepping up, plus corroborating photos. DLQI (7→8→15→17) and the new nail/scalp findings from May onward are documented with quotes. But criteria 4 and 5 are both marked insufficient, and the whole worsening attribution rests on an assumption nobody has verified: that the patient was actually taking the biologic during the interval when he got worse. The chart defers adherence to a separate review not in the packet. The sharp deterioration lands between the March and May visits — I'm pulling the pharmacy fills to see whether coverage was continuous across that gap, because if it …
Acting as the nurse, we called up the pharmacy's dispense records to see what was actually filled — 5 record(s) on file.
Ver
ification notes: The pharmacy feed tells the story. The biologic is adalimumab, dosed every 2 wee
ks. He filled it three times — Jan 19, Feb 16, and Mar 16 — each a 28-day supply. The
last biologic dispense (Mar 16) covers through about Apr 13. There is NO adalimumab refill after that:
nothing before the May 10 visit and nothing before June 29. That is exactly the window where
PASI jumped from 6.3 to 14.2 to 16.8. So the sharp worsening overlaps a
biologic coverage gap of roughly two months. That makes non-adherence a live, unexcluded confounder for the most
recent deterioration — the worsening can't be pinned on the disease itself until we know he
was still on drug while he got worse. (The June 15 clobetasol refill is topical steroid, not the biologic, and doesn't close the gap.) Filing under the

confounder criterion; the measurements themselves aren't in dispute, the attribution is.

Verification notes: The pharmacy feed tells the story. The biologic is adalimumab, dosed every 2 weeks. He filled it three times — Jan 19, Feb 16, and Mar 16 — each a 28-day supply. The last biologic dispense (Mar 16) covers through about Apr 13. There is NO adalimumab refill after that: nothing before the May 10 visit and nothing before June 29. That is exactly the window where PASI jumped from 6.3 to 14.2 to 16.8. So the sharp worsening overlaps a biologic coverage gap of roughly two months. That makes non-adherence a live, unexcluded confounder for the most recent deterioration — the worsening can't be pinned on the disease itself until we know he was still on drug while he got worse. …

On question 5: The pharmacy records show the patient's biologic injection, adalimumab, was last filled on March 16 with a 28-day supply, so his coverage ran out around April 13. There's no refill after that before either the May 10 or the June 29 visit. That's exactly the stretch where his psoriasis got sharply worse. So we can't …

data.rubric_quote: Worsening cannot be attributed to disease if a confounder (non-adherence, infection, steroid-withdrawal or seasonal flare) is unaddressed.
data.what_would_satisfy: Show us the severity kept climbing during a stretch when he was confirmed to be taking his injections on schedule, or acknowledge that we can't separate the worsening from the two months he went without his biologic refill.

external_evidence.detail: last adalimumab dispense 2026-03-16, 28-day supply; coverage ended approx 2026-04-13; no subsequent adalimumab fill before the 2026-05-10 or 2026-06-29 visits, spanning the interval of PASI rise 6.3->14.2->16.8
2026-06-15 dispense is clobetasol topical steroid, not the biologic; does not close the adalimumab gap

Hi from your skin care team. Your psoriasis looks more active at your recent visits, but it seems there was a gap in your adalimumab (Humira) shots, so the picture isn't fully clear. Please restart it as prescribed, and reply with about when the flare-up started. We're here to help. — your dermatology care team
